title: Variance, Covariance & LLN
subtitle: Spread, joint movement, and convergence in the limit
intro: Variance measures how far a random variable tends to spread around its mean: low variance means outcomes cluster tightly; high variance means wide swings. The standard deviation is the square root of the variance and shares the same units as the original variable, which makes it easier to interpret in practice.

Covariance captures whether two variables move together (positive), move in opposite directions (negative), or show no linear relationship (zero). Correlation rescales covariance to a number between -1 and 1. The law of large numbers (LLN) says that as you repeat independent trials, the sample average converges to the true expected value — the foundation of Monte Carlo methods and statistical estimation in quant interviews.

Variance and the law of large numbers bridge classroom probability and real quant work: risk is spread, portfolios combine covariances, and Monte Carlo averages converge because sample means stabilize. Stat 110 pairs Var(X) computations with the LLN story—repeat independent trials and noise in the average shrinks. Interviewers love asking whether diversification lowers portfolio variance or why a simulation with 10 draws looks noisy but 10,000 draws does not.

questions.question: Which formula correctly defines the variance Var(X) of a random variable X with mean μ?

explanations.A: Correct. Variance is the expected squared deviation from the mean, E[(X − μ)²], which measures how far outcomes typically fall from μ.
explanations.B: E[X]² is just the squared mean — a measure of location, not spread. It can be large even when X never varies.
explanations.C: (E[X])² is also the square of the mean, a fixed constant that says nothing about how X disperses around μ.
explanations.D: This mixes moments incorrectly. The shortcut is E[X²] − (E[X])², squaring the full mean, not subtracting E[X] itself.

remediation.question: Variance measures how far values tend to spread from the:

explanations.A: Correct. Variance is the average squared distance of outcomes from the mean μ, so the mean is its reference point.
explanations.B: Spread in variance is measured from the mean, not the median. The median is a different center used in other robustness measures.
explanations.C: The maximum is a single extreme value, not the balancing center that variance measures deviations from.
explanations.D: The mode is the most frequent value; variance is defined around the mean, not the mode.

remediation.question: A distribution with very little spread around its center has:

explanations.A: Correct. Tightly clustered outcomes have small squared deviations from the mean, so the variance is low.
explanations.B: High variance describes wide swings around the mean — the opposite of a tightly clustered distribution.
explanations.C: Infinite variance arises only for heavy-tailed distributions, not for a tightly concentrated one.
explanations.D: Variance is an average of squared terms, so it can never be negative.

questions.question: Which identity is always true for any random variable X?

explanations.A: Correct. Expanding E[(X − μ)²] gives E[X²] − (E[X])², the standard computational formula for variance.
explanations.B: Adding the moments would inflate the result; variance subtracts the squared mean, it does not add it.
explanations.C: This reverses the subtraction. (E[X])² − E[X²] is the negative of the variance and would be ≤ 0.
explanations.D: E[X] − E[X²] mixes the first and second moments with no squaring of the mean, so it is not a valid variance identity.

remediation.question: The computational shortcut Var(X) = E[X²] − (E[X])² is often called the:

explanations.A: Correct. This is the computational (shortcut) formula for variance, derived by expanding E[(X − μ)²].
explanations.B: Covariance involves two variables, Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] − E[X]E[Y], not a single variable's spread.
explanations.C: Bayes rule relates conditional probabilities; it has nothing to do with computing variance.
explanations.D: The LLN describes convergence of sample averages, not a moment-based variance identity.

remediation.question: If E[X] = 3 and E[X²] = 13, then Var(X) equals:

explanations.A: Correct. Var(X) = E[X²] − (E[X])² = 13 − 3² = 13 − 9 = 4.
explanations.B: 10 comes from 13 − 3, subtracting E[X] instead of (E[X])² = 9.
explanations.C: 16 adds the moments (13 + 3) or uses 4², neither of which is the variance.
explanations.D: 9 is (E[X])² alone; you must subtract it from E[X²], not report it as the variance.

questions.question: If a stock return is measured in percent, its standard deviation is also measured in:

explanations.A: Correct. Taking the square root of variance restores the original units, so a return in percent has a standard deviation in percent.
explanations.B: Percent squared is the unit of the variance, not the standard deviation, which un-squares those units.
explanations.C: Standard deviation keeps the units of the variable; it is not dimensionless. (Correlation is the dimensionless quantity.)
explanations.D: Dollars would apply to a P&L measured in dollars, not to a return already expressed in percent.

remediation.question: Standard deviation is defined as the ___ of the variance.

explanations.A: Correct. SD(X) = √Var(X), which puts the spread back into the variable's original units.
explanations.B: Squaring the variance would give units to the fourth power — the wrong direction.
explanations.C: The reciprocal of variance has no standard interpretation as spread.
explanations.D: Logarithms are unrelated to the definition of standard deviation.

remediation.question: If Var(X) = 9, then the standard deviation SD(X) equals:

explanations.A: Correct. SD(X) = √9 = 3.
explanations.B: 9 is the variance itself; standard deviation is its square root.
explanations.C: 81 is 9², which squares the variance instead of taking its root.
explanations.D: 1.5 is 9/6 or half of 3; neither equals √9.

questions.question: If X is a constant (always equal to c), what is Var(X)?

explanations.A: Correct. A constant never deviates from its mean (which is c), so every squared deviation is 0 and Var(X) = 0.
explanations.B: c is the value (and the mean) of X, not its spread. A constant has no spread.
explanations.C: c² is the square of the value; variance measures deviation from the mean, which is zero here.
explanations.D: 1 is an arbitrary nonzero number; a constant has exactly zero variance.

remediation.question: A random variable with no randomness at all has variance equal to:

explanations.A: Correct. With no randomness, outcomes never deviate from the mean, so the variance is 0.
explanations.B: 1 implies some spread; a degenerate (non-random) variable has none.
explanations.C: The mean describes the value, not the spread, which is zero.
explanations.D: The mean squared is still a location-based quantity, not the zero spread of a constant.

remediation.question: If every outcome of X is exactly 7, then SD(X) equals:

explanations.A: Correct. X is constant at 7, so Var(X) = 0 and SD(X) = √0 = 0.
explanations.B: 7 is the value of X, not its spread. A fixed value has no deviation.
explanations.C: 49 is 7²; squaring the value does not give standard deviation, which is zero here.
explanations.D: 1 implies variability, but X never changes, so its standard deviation is 0.

questions.question: If Cov(X, Y) > 0, which statement best describes the relationship between X and Y?
options: They always move in opposite directions

explanations.A: Correct. Positive covariance means that when X is above its mean, Y tends to be above its mean too — they move together on average.
explanations.B: Opposite movement corresponds to negative covariance, not positive.
explanations.C: Independence forces Cov(X,Y) = 0, not a positive value.
explanations.D: A constant Y has zero variance and zero covariance with anything, contradicting Cov > 0.

remediation.question: Positive covariance means that when X is above its mean, Y tends to be:

explanations.A: Correct. Positive covariance pairs above-mean X with above-mean Y, so the deviation products are positive on average.
explanations.B: Y below its mean while X is above would produce negative covariance.
explanations.C: Y being exactly zero is unrelated to the sign of covariance; covariance compares deviations from means.
explanations.D: A constant Y has zero covariance, not positive.

remediation.question: If Cov(X, Y) < 0, then X and Y tend to move in:

explanations.A: Correct. Negative covariance means above-mean X pairs with below-mean Y, so they tend to move in opposite directions.
explanations.B: Moving in the same direction is positive covariance.
explanations.C: No pattern at all corresponds to zero covariance, not negative.
explanations.D: Perfect lockstep is correlation +1 (positive covariance), the opposite of this case.

questions.question: If X and Y are independent random variables, what must Cov(X, Y) equal?

explanations.A: Correct. Independence gives E[XY] = E[X]E[Y], so Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] − E[X]E[Y] = 0.
explanations.B: Covariance is not normalized to 1; only correlation is bounded, and independence drives covariance to 0.
explanations.C: Var(X) is the covariance of X with itself, not the covariance between two independent variables.
explanations.D: Var(Y) is the self-covariance of Y; independent variables share no covariance, so the answer is 0.

remediation.question: Independence implies that knowing X gives you no information about Y. Their covariance is:

explanations.A: Correct. Independent variables have no linear association, so their covariance is exactly 0.
explanations.B: A positive value would indicate they move together — that contradicts independence.
explanations.C: A negative value would indicate opposite movement, which independence rules out.
explanations.D: E[X]E[Y] is subtracted from E[XY] in the covariance formula; under independence those terms cancel to give 0.

remediation.question: Two fair dice rolls are independent. The covariance between the two results is:

explanations.A: Correct. Separate dice rolls are independent, so their covariance is 0.
explanations.B: 1 would imply a relationship between the rolls; independent dice have none.
explanations.C: 3.5 is the expected value of a single die, not the covariance between two rolls.
explanations.D: 7 is the expected sum of two dice, not their covariance.

questions.question: The correlation coefficient ρ(X, Y) always lies in which interval?

explanations.A: Correct. Dividing covariance by SD(X)·SD(Y) (Cauchy–Schwarz) bounds correlation to [−1, 1].
explanations.B: [0, 1] omits negative correlation, but variables can move in opposite directions (ρ down to −1).
explanations.C: Correlation is normalized, so it cannot be unbounded like raw covariance.
explanations.D: [0, ∞] both excludes negatives and ignores the upper bound of +1.

remediation.question: Correlation is defined as Cov(X, Y) divided by:

explanations.A: Correct. ρ = Cov(X,Y) / (SD(X)·SD(Y)), which rescales covariance into [−1, 1].
explanations.B: Summing variances does not normalize the units the way the product of standard deviations does.
explanations.C: Dividing by E[X]·E[Y] uses means, not the spread; that is not how correlation is defined.
explanations.D: The denominator is the product, not the sum, of the standard deviations.

remediation.question: A correlation of +1 means the two variables have a:

explanations.A: Correct. ρ = +1 means Y is an exact increasing linear function of X — they lie on an upward-sloping line.
explanations.B: No relationship corresponds to ρ = 0, not +1.
explanations.C: A perfect negative relationship is ρ = −1.
explanations.D: Correlation measures only linear association; ρ = +1 is the strongest positive linear case, not a nonlinear one.

questions.question: The weak law of large numbers states that as n → ∞, the sample mean X̄ₙ converges to:

explanations.A: Correct. The WLLN says the sample mean converges in probability to the true expected value E[X].
explanations.B: Var(X) describes spread; the sample mean targets the center E[X], not the variance.
explanations.C: The limit is 0 only when E[X] happens to be 0 — the general target is E[X].
explanations.D: The sample maximum grows with extreme draws; it is not the limit of the average.

remediation.question: As you flip a fair coin more and more times, the fraction of heads converges to:

explanations.A: Correct. By the LLN, the sample proportion of heads converges to the true probability p = 0.5.
explanations.B: 0 would mean heads essentially never occur, contradicting a fair coin.
explanations.C: 1 would mean heads always occur, again impossible for a fair coin.
explanations.D: The whole point of the LLN is that the long-run fraction stabilizes, not that it stays random.

remediation.question: The LLN explains why Monte Carlo estimates improve as you:

explanations.A: Correct. More independent draws shrink the estimator's variance (σ²/n), so the average converges to the true value.
explanations.B: Fewer simulations give a noisier estimate — the opposite of improvement.
explanations.C: Monte Carlo relies on randomness; ignoring it defeats the method.
explanations.D: A single trial has maximal sampling error; averaging many trials is what helps.

questions.question: If X and Y are independent with Var(X) = 4 and Var(Y) = 9, what is Var(X + Y)?

explanations.A: Correct. For independent variables Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) = 4 + 9 = 13 (the covariance term is 0).
explanations.B: 5 is 9 − 4; variances of a sum add, they do not subtract.
explanations.C: 36 multiplies the variances (4 × 9); variance of a sum adds them.
explanations.D: 6.5 averages the variances, which is not the rule for the variance of a sum.

remediation.question: For independent X and Y, variances of sums add: Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y). If Var(X) = 2 and Var(Y) = 3, then Var(X + Y) =

explanations.A: Correct. 2 + 3 = 5, since independence makes the covariance term vanish.
explanations.B: 1 is 3 − 2; variances add for a sum, they do not subtract.
explanations.C: 6 multiplies 2 × 3 instead of adding.
explanations.D: 2.5 averages the two variances rather than summing them.

remediation.question: If X and Y are independent, Cov(X, Y) = 0, so Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) + 2·Cov(X,Y) simplifies to:

explanations.A: Correct. With Cov(X,Y) = 0 the cross term drops out, leaving Var(X) + Var(Y).
explanations.B: Subtraction would only appear for Var(X − Y) with a negative covariance, not here.
explanations.C: Variances combine additively, not multiplicatively.
explanations.D: 0 would require both variances to vanish; dropping the covariance term still leaves Var(X) + Var(Y).

questions.question: Two assets each have variance 0.04 (so σ = 0.2) and correlation ρ = 0.5. For an equal-weight portfolio (w = 0.5 each), the portfolio variance w²σ₁² + w²σ₂² + 2w²·ρσ₁σ₂ equals:

explanations.B: 0.04 is the variance of a single asset; combining two correlated assets with equal weights lowers it to 0.03.
explanations.C: 0.06 forgets the w² = 0.25 weighting on the variance terms, inflating the result.
explanations.D: 0.02 keeps only the two weighted variance terms (0.01 + 0.01) and drops the positive covariance contribution.

remediation.question: For two assets with equal weights w = 0.5, portfolio variance includes a cross term 2w²·Cov. If Cov = 0 (independent), portfolio variance with equal weights and equal variances σ² each is:

explanations.A: Correct. 0.25σ² + 0.25σ² = 0.5σ² = σ²/2 — diversification across two independent assets halves the variance.
explanations.B: σ² is the variance of a single asset; equal-weighting two independent assets reduces it.
explanations.C: 2σ² would be the variance of the unweighted sum, not the equal-weight average.
explanations.D: 0 would require the assets to have no variance at all, which is not the case.

remediation.question: Diversification reduces portfolio risk when asset returns have correlation:

explanations.A: Correct. Any ρ < 1 makes the combined volatility less than the weighted average, so imperfect correlation creates a diversification benefit.
explanations.B: At ρ = 1 the assets move in lockstep and there is no risk reduction from combining them.
explanations.C: Correlation cannot exceed 1, so this case is impossible.
explanations.D: ρ = −1 maximizes the benefit, but diversification helps for any correlation below 1, not only at −1.

questions.question: Let X be Bernoulli(p) with p = 0.25. What is Var(X)?

explanations.B: 0.25 is p itself, not the variance.
explanations.C: 0.75 is 1 − p, the failure probability.
explanations.D: 0.0625 equals p² = (0.25)², not p(1−p).

remediation.question: For Bernoulli(p), Var(X) equals:

explanations.A: Correct. Standard Bernoulli variance formula.
explanations.B: p² is not the variance of a Bernoulli trial.
explanations.C: 1 − p is the failure probability, not variance.
explanations.D: p is the mean, not the variance.

remediation.question: Bernoulli(p = 0.5) has variance:

explanations.B: 0.5 is the mean, not variance.
explanations.C: 0 would mean no randomness, but a fair coin flip varies.
explanations.D: 1 exceeds the maximum Bernoulli variance of 0.25 at p = 0.5.

questions.question: X₁, …, Xₙ are i.i.d. with mean μ and finite variance. As n grows, the sample mean X̄ₙ satisfies:
options: X̄ₙ converges to μ (law of large numbers)
X̄ₙ always equals μ for any finite n

explanations.A: Correct. The LLN says sample averages stabilize around the true mean as n increases.
explanations.B: Var(X̄ₙ) = σ²/n shrinks as n grows, it does not stay constant.
explanations.C: For finite n, random fluctuation remains; equality holds only in the limit.
explanations.D: Averages of bounded-mean variables do not diverge under the LLN assumptions.

remediation.question: If Var(X) = σ² and X̄ₙ is the mean of n i.i.d. copies, Var(X̄ₙ) equals:

explanations.A: Correct. Averaging divides variance by n.
explanations.B: σ² is the variance of one observation, not the mean.
explanations.C: nσ² multiplies instead of divides.
explanations.D: σ/√n is the standard error, not the variance.

remediation.question: Monte Carlo pricing averages many simulated payoffs because the LLN guarantees:
options: The sample average approximates the true expected payoff
Each simulation equals the exact price

explanations.A: Correct. More independent draws make the sample mean a better estimate of E[payoff].
explanations.B: Individual simulations vary; only the average stabilizes.
explanations.C: More simulations reduce variance of the estimate, not increase it.
explanations.D: Independence (or appropriate ergodicity) is essential for the LLN logic.

explanations.A: Correct. Variance is an average of squared deviations, so it is always ≥ 0.
explanations.B: A squared quantity can never be negative, so variance cannot be negative.
explanations.C: Variance is zero only for a constant; in general it is positive.
explanations.D: Variance measures spread and is unrelated to the value of the mean.

explanations.A: Correct. SD(X) = √25 = 5.
explanations.B: 25 is the variance itself, not its square root.
explanations.C: 625 is 25², squaring instead of rooting.
explanations.D: 12.5 is half of 25, not √25.

placementQuestions.question: Var(c) for a constant c equals:

explanations.A: Correct. A constant never deviates from its mean, so Var(c) = 0.
explanations.B: c is the value of the constant, not its spread.
explanations.C: c² is the squared value, not the variance of a fixed number.
explanations.D: 1 implies variability, but a constant has none.

placementQuestions.question: Negative covariance means X and Y tend to:

explanations.A: Correct. Negative covariance pairs above-mean X with below-mean Y, so they move oppositely.
explanations.B: Moving together is positive covariance.
explanations.C: Independence gives zero covariance, not negative.
explanations.D: Being equal would make them perfectly positively related, not negatively.

explanations.A: Correct. Independence makes E[XY] = E[X]E[Y], so Cov(X,Y) = 0.
explanations.B: Covariance is not normalized to 1; independence drives it to 0.
explanations.C: Var(X) is the self-covariance of X, not the covariance between independent variables.
explanations.D: E[XY] is only one term of the covariance; under independence it cancels with E[X]E[Y] to give 0.

explanations.A: Correct. Correlation is bounded in [−1, 1] by the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality.
explanations.B: This excludes negative correlation, which is possible down to −1.
explanations.C: ρ can be negative, so ρ ≥ 0 is not always true.
explanations.D: ρ = 0 holds only for uncorrelated variables, not in general.

placementQuestions.question: The sample mean converges to the population mean by the:

explanations.A: Correct. The law of large numbers guarantees the sample mean converges to E[X].
explanations.B: The CLT describes the shape (normality) of the scaled error, not the convergence of the mean itself.
explanations.C: Bayes theorem updates probabilities; it does not describe sample-mean convergence.
explanations.D: The Markov property concerns memorylessness of processes, not sample averages.

placementQuestions.question: If X and Y are independent, Var(X + Y) =

explanations.A: Correct. Independence zeroes the covariance term, so variances of a sum simply add.
explanations.B: Subtraction is not the rule for summing independent variables.
explanations.C: Variances combine additively, not by multiplication.
explanations.D: 0 would require both variances to be zero; in general the sum is Var(X) + Var(Y).
